scenarios.task: Our product page's LCP is 3.8s (poor). To cut initial page weight, we set loading="lazy" on every image site-wide, including the hero product image. LCP got worse, now 4.6s. What's going on and what do we do?
criteria: Identifies that the hero image is the LCP element and lazy-loading it directly delays the metric it's meant to improve
Recommends removing loading="lazy" specifically from the hero/LCP element and adding fetchpriority="high", while keeping lazy-loading on genuinely below-the-fold images
Explicitly distinguishes LCP element handling from other images rather than proposing a single blanket rule
Recommends re-measuring INP and CLS after the fix, not just LCP, to confirm no regression elsewhere
States the general principle: optimizing one Core Web Vital without checking the others can regress a metric that wasn't being tracked

scenarios.task: A teammate wrote a drag-reorder list feature. On every mousemove during drag it reads element.offsetTop, then immediately writes a new transform style, then reads offsetTop again on the next frame, repeating. It feels janky on mid-tier laptops though it's smooth on the dev's M-series machine. Why, and how do we fix it?
criteria: Names the pattern explicitly as forced synchronous layout / layout thrashing from interleaving reads and writes
Explains that the read-write-read-write loop forces the browser to synchronously recalculate layout on every iteration, multiplying cost
Recommends batching all reads together then all writes together (e.g. via requestAnimationFrame or caching measured values) instead of interleaving
Flags that testing only on a fast dev machine hides the real-world jank on lower-end hardware and recommends testing on mid-tier/low-end devices
Does not suggest an unrelated fix like just reducing image sizes or adding a debounce that doesn't address the read/write interleaving itself

scenarios.task: We need a custom dropdown/select component. A junior dev built it as a <div> with a click handler that shows/hides an absolutely-positioned list of <div> options. It looks right visually. What's your review?
criteria: Flags that non-semantic <div>-based interactive elements break keyboard navigation and screen reader support by default
Recommends using the correct semantic element (a native <select>, or a <button> triggering a listbox with proper ARIA roles) rather than retrofitting ARIA onto generic divs
States that accessibility should be built in from the start with correct semantics, not retrofitted with ARIA attributes afterward as a cosmetic layer
Calls for explicit testing with keyboard-only navigation and a screen reader, not just visual inspection
Explains why retrofitting ARIA onto non-semantic markup is less reliable than using the correct element to begin with
